The willingness to put out your own eyes rather than unsee what you have seen — this is not pathology. It is the highest form of intellectual honesty.

VII. The Hesiodic and the Oedipal — Together

What connects Hesiod’s cosmogony to Sophocles’s tragedy?

Both are stories about the violence of origins — about what must be destroyed for anything to come into being.

In Hesiod: the cosmos requires castration, swallowing, defeat. The world as it is is built on the ruins of what it destroyed to become itself.

In Sophocles: knowledge requires the destruction of the self that did not know. The Oedipus who knows at the end of the play is not the Oedipus who began it. He has been destroyed and remade — at the cost of his eyes, his throne, his family, his city.

Both stories say: you cannot become without destruction.

The cosmos cannot be born without the father being unmade.

The truth cannot be known without the knower being transformed by what they learn.

This is the deepest mythological wisdom:

Origin is violence. Knowledge is violence. Becoming is violence — not in the sense of cruelty, but in the sense of irreversibility.

What has been destroyed to make you cannot be restored.

The eyes that saw Ouranos castrated — the ears that heard the oracle — cannot unremember.

The only question is: what do you do with the irreversibility?

VIII. The Mythological Answer

Greek mythology does not offer comfort.

It does not say: the violence was worth it. It does not say: everything happens for a reason. It does not say: the suffering will be redeemed.

It says: this is the structure of things.

Chaos opens. Generation begins. Each generation destroys and is destroyed. The cosmos is built on castration. Knowledge is built on self-blinding. The order we inhabit is the product of violences we did not choose and cannot undo.

And we live here. In this order. With these origins. Carrying these wounds.

The Theogony does not ask us to overcome our origins. It asks us to know them.

Oedipus does not ask us to avoid the truth. It asks us to follow it — wherever it leads — including into darkness.

This is the mythological answer:

Not redemption. Recognition.

The courageous act is not to transcend the violence at the origin of things. It is to look at it clearly — with eyes that have earned their sight through the willingness to see — and say:

Yes. This is where I come from. This is what made me. I know it now. I will not look away.

IX. What We Carry

We are all Oedipal in the mythological sense — not in Freud’s narrower sense, but in the sense that Sophocles intended:

We are all the products of origins we did not choose and cannot fully know.

We are all carrying wounds inflicted before we could consent to them — family wounds, cultural wounds, historical wounds, the wounds of being born into a specific time and place and body.

And we are all, like Oedipus, confronted with the choice:

To stop asking. To accept the comfortable not-knowing. To live in the city of Thebes without looking too closely at how it was built.

Or to ask. To follow the question. To risk what Oedipus risked — the discovery that the investigator is implicated in what they investigate, that the wound and the healer are the same person, that knowing changes nothing about what happened and everything about who you are in relation to what happened.

Tiresias warned Oedipus. He did not listen.

I think Sophocles admired him for it.

I do too.

X. Hesiod’s Last Word

The Theogony ends not with resolution but with catalogue — the endless list of gods and their children, the proliferation of divine genealogy that is the cosmos’s way of sustaining itself through multiplication.

There is something honest about this ending.

The cosmos does not resolve. It generates. It continues. It produces more of itself — more gods, more heroes, more stories, more attempts to understand the original gap that opened before anything was.

Chaos — the first word. The gap that is not nothing but the condition of everything.

We come from this.

Not from order, not from intention, not from a god who shaped us with a clear purpose in mind —

but from an opening, a gap, an abyss that could not sustain its own emptiness and began, from that unbearable fullness of nothing, to generate.

We are the cosmos knowing itself.

Imperfectly. Painfully. With eyes that sometimes need to be put out before they can truly see.

But knowing.

That is what we are. That is what we do.

That is, perhaps, enough.

Further reading: Hesiod — Theogony and Works and Days (trans. M.L. West — Oxford World’s Classics) Sophocles — The Three Theban Plays (trans. Robert Fagles — Penguin Classics) Walter Burkert — Greek Religion (1985) — the essential scholarly introduction Jean-Pierre Vernant — Myth and Thought Among the Greeks (1983) — the best structural analysis of Greek mythology Bernard Knox — The Heroic Temper (1964) — on Oedipus and the will to know Roberto Calasso — The Marriage of Cadmus and Harmony (1993) — mythology as literature; the most beautiful book about Greek myth
entry.summary: On Hesiod's cosmogony, the violence at the origin of things, and why the myth of Oedipus is not about incest but about the unbearable desire to know.

entry.title: What Is Time? — Augustine, Heidegger, and the Physics of the Passing Moment

link.title: What Is Time? — Augustine, Heidegger, and the Physics of the Passing Moment

entry.content:
Augustine of Hippo, in the year 397, wrote the most honest sentence in the history of philosophy:

“Quid est ergo tempus? Si nemo ex me quaerat, scio; si quaerenti explicare velim, nescio.”

“What then is time? If no one asks me, I know. If I want to explain it to someone who asks, I do not know.”

He wrote this in the Confessions — a book that is itself a meditation on time, on memory, on the self that persists through the passing of moments.

He is right.

You know what time is in the way you know how to breathe — without thinking about it, without being able to explain it.

The moment you try to explain it, the thing dissolves.

The present: where is it? By the time you name it, it is gone. The past: it no longer exists. The future: it does not yet exist.

And yet time is the most real thing there is. More real than space. More intimate than thought.

You are inside it right now. You cannot get out. You cannot examine it from outside.

This is the problem of time.

I. Augustine — Time as the Soul’s Extension

Augustine’s analysis of time in Book XI of the Confessions is the first great philosophical account of temporal experience.

His starting point: the cosmological question.

“What was God doing before the Creation?”

The naive answer: preparing hell for those who ask such questions. Augustine quotes this with gentle irony.

His real answer: the question is malformed.

Before the Creation, there was no time. Time was created with the world. “Before” requires time. There is no before.

“Tempus autem non fuisset, nisi creatura fieret.”

“Time would not exist, unless a creature were made.”

This is a profound move: time is not the container in which events occur. Time is itself created — it is part of the universe, not the stage on which the universe performs.

Augustine anticipates, by 1500 years, Einstein’s insight that time and space are not prior to physics but are themselves physical.

The three presents:

Augustine’s most famous contribution to the philosophy of time:

The past does not exist — it is gone. The future does not exist — it has not yet come. The present is a knife-edge — it passes before you can name it.

And yet: we experience all three.

How?

Through the soul.

“Praesens de praeteritis memoria, praesens de praesentibus contuitus, praesens de futuris expectatio.”

“The present of past things is memory, the present of present things is sight, the present of future things is expectation.”

The three modes of time are three modes of the soul’s attention:

Memory — the past held in the present. Attention — the present grasped in the present. Expectation — the future anticipated in the present.

All three are present. All three are in the soul. Time is not out there in the world — it is in here, in the mind that holds past and future in its present attention.

“In te, anime meus, tempora metior.”

“In you, my soul, I measure time.”

This is Augustine’s radical claim: time is psychological. Not in the sense that it is unreal — but in the sense that temporal experience is constituted by the mind’s activity of holding past, present, and future together.

The distension of the soul:

Augustine uses the word distentio — distension, stretching — to describe the soul’s temporal structure.

The soul is stretched across time — pulled toward the past by memory, pulled toward the future by expectation, held at the present by attention.

This stretching is not comfortable. It is the source of anxiety — of the restlessness that Augustine describes in the Confessions’ most famous line:

“Inquietum est cor nostrum, donec requiescat in te.”

“Our heart is restless, until it rests in you.”

The restlessness is temporal. The soul stretched across past and future — unable to rest in the present — is the condition of temporal existence.

Rest — the Sabbath, eternity — is the release from distension.

II. Heidegger — Being-Toward-Death

Martin Heidegger’s Being and Time (1927) is the most ambitious philosophical work of the twentieth century — an attempt to ask, for the first time since the Greeks, the fundamental question:

What does it mean to be?

And his answer: to understand being, you must understand time. Because being is temporal. Everything that is — is in time.

Heidegger’s fundamental concept: Dasein — being-there — the kind of being that we are.

Dasein is not a subject contemplating an object. It is not a mind inside a body. It is being-in-the-world — already thrown into a situation, already caring about things, already engaged before reflection begins.

Thrownness:

We did not choose to exist. We did not choose our time, our place, our language, our body. We found ourselves thrown into a situation not of our choosing.

This is Geworfenheit — thrownness. The facticity of existence: that I am here, now, this — without having chosen any of it.

The temporality of Dasein:

Dasein is always: — Coming from a past it did not choose (thrownness) — Moving toward a future it cannot avoid (projection) — Existing in a present shaped by both

This is the temporal structure of human existence: ecstatic temporality — the “ek-stasis” (standing-outside) of existing always beyond the present moment, always relating to what was and what will be.

Being-toward-death:

Heidegger’s most radical claim:

Death is not an event that happens at the end. Death is a structure of existence — now.

I am always being-toward-death — always living in the light of my finitude, always existing as the being for whom my existence is an issue.

This is what distinguishes Dasein from a stone or a tree: not consciousness, exactly — but the fact that my being matters to me, that I must take a stand on it, that I cannot be indifferent to whether I exist.

Authentic and inauthentic existence:

Most of the time, we flee from the awareness of finitude.

We lose ourselves in the das Man — the “they,” the anonymous public — doing what one does, thinking what one thinks, living as one lives.

This is inauthenticity — not moral failure, but the everyday flight from the anxiety of genuine selfhood.

Authentic existence: confronting death, owning your thrownness, choosing from the possibilities that are genuinely yours — not because they are chosen for you by the anonymous they.

Anxiety:

For Heidegger, the mood that reveals the true structure of existence is Angst — anxiety.

Not fear of a specific thing. Anxiety is anxiety about nothing — about existence itself, about the groundlessness of being, about the fact that I am here without justification or guarantee.

Anxiety strips away the comfortable securities of everyday life and reveals what is always already there: the finite, temporal, groundless structure of human existence.

This is not pathology. It is clarity.

“Das Sein zum Tode enthüllt eine eigenste Möglichkeit des Daseins.”

“Being-toward-death discloses the ownmost possibility of Dasein.”

III. The Physics of Time — What Science Says

Philosophy asks: what is time? Physics answers — partially, strangely, in ways that deepen the mystery rather than resolve it.

Newton’s absolute time:

For Newton, time is absolute, true, and mathematical — flowing equably, without relation to anything external.

It is the background — the container — in which events occur.

The same time passes for everyone, everywhere. There is a universal now.

This is what common sense tells us. It is wrong.

Special relativity — the relativity of simultaneity:

Einstein showed (1905): simultaneity is relative. Two events simultaneous in one reference frame are not simultaneous in another.

There is no universal now. The “same moment” means nothing without specifying a reference frame.

Time dilation: moving clocks run slow. A clock on a fast-moving spaceship ticks more slowly than a clock at rest. Not as an illusion — really. The astronaut who travels at near-light-speed ages more slowly than the person who stays.

Time is not absolute. It is woven into spacetime — a four-dimensional manifold in which time and space are related by the Lorentz transformation.

General relativity — time curves:

In the presence of mass and energy, spacetime curves. Time itself curves.

Gravitational time dilation: clocks run slower in stronger gravitational fields. A clock at sea level ticks more slowly than a clock on a mountain.

Not by much — but measurably. GPS satellites must account for this.

Near a black hole: time slows toward the event horizon. At the horizon: time stops — from the perspective of a distant observer.

The block universe:

The most radical implication of relativity:

The block universe — or eternalism — the view that past, present, and future all equally exist.

In special relativity, different observers disagree about which events are simultaneous. There is no preferred “now” that carves the four-dimensional spacetime into past and future.

If there is no preferred now — if “now” is relative to the observer — then the distinction between “things that exist (present)” and “things that no longer exist (past)” is also observer-dependent.

The most natural interpretation: all moments equally exist. The past still exists. The future already exists.

We experience time as flowing — as having a present, a past, a future — but this experience may not correspond to the structure of the world.

The world may be a static four-dimensional block. The flow of time may be an illusion — a feature of our experience, not a feature of reality.

The arrow of time:

If the laws of physics are time-symmetric — if the equations of QM and GR look the same run forwards and backwards —

why does time have a direction?

Why do we remember the past but not the future? Why do broken eggs not reassemble? Why does entropy increase?

The arrow of time comes from: the second law of thermodynamics — entropy increases toward the future.

But this requires explanation: why was entropy low in the past?

The answer: the Big Bang was an extremely low-entropy state. The universe began in an improbable configuration. Everything since has been moving toward greater entropy — toward greater disorder — and this is the direction we call “future.”

The arrow of time points toward increasing entropy — and entropy was low at the beginning because of initial conditions we cannot explain from within the physics.

The Planck time:

At \(t_P \approx 5.39 \times 10^{-44}\) seconds — the Planck time — our physics breaks down.

Before the Planck time after the Big Bang: the concept of time may not apply.

Quantum gravity — which we do not have — is needed to describe what “time” means at the Planck scale.

Whether time is continuous or discrete, whether it had a beginning, whether it will have an end — these are questions our physics cannot currently answer.

IV. The Experience of Time — Phenomenology

Between Augustine’s psychological time and physics’ geometrical time, there is a third perspective: the phenomenology of temporal experience.

Husserl’s time-consciousness:

Edmund Husserl — Heidegger’s teacher — analyzed the structure of the “living present” in On the Phenomenology of the Consciousness of Internal Time (1928).

The present moment, for Husserl, is not a knife-edge — an instantaneous now with no duration.

It is a specious present — a small stretch of time that is experienced as simultaneous.

Within this living present: retention — the just-past, still held in the present experience — and protention — the about-to-come, already anticipated.

When you hear a melody, you hear the notes as a sequence — not as isolated sounds. You hear them as a melody because the earlier notes are retained while the later notes are heard.

The present moment of hearing contains, within it, the retained past and the anticipated future.

This is why music is possible. This is why speech is possible. Without retention and protention, each moment would be completely isolated — no connection to what came before, no anticipation of what comes next.

Time-consciousness makes experience coherent.

The phenomenology of boredom:

Heidegger, in his 1929-30 lecture course The Fundamental Concepts of Metaphysics, analyzes boredom as a fundamental mood that reveals the structure of time.

When bored, time slows — conspicuously. You become aware of time’s passing precisely because it is not passing.

The structure revealed: time is normally transparent — we live through it without noticing it, absorbed in our activities.

Boredom makes time opaque — suddenly visible, heavy, resistant.

The bored person is held by time, unable to fill it, unable to move through it, exposed to its pure passing.

This is why boredom is so uncomfortable — it exposes the temporality of existence that absorption normally conceals.

The phenomenology of flow:

Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi’s concept of flow — the state of complete absorption in a challenging activity — is the opposite of boredom.

In flow: time disappears. The person is entirely in the present — no past, no future, no awareness of duration.

Athletes, musicians, programmers, writers in the state of deep work — all report the disappearance of time.

Two hours pass in what feels like twenty minutes. Or twenty minutes feel like two hours.

The experience of time’s duration is not measured by the clock — it is constituted by the quality of attention.

Full attention: time disappears. Empty attention: time expands unbearably.

V. Memory — The Past That Lives

Augustine said: the present of past things is memory.

Memory is not a recording. It is a reconstruction.

The reconstructive nature of memory:

Every time you remember something, you reconstruct it — from fragments, from expectations, from your current emotional state, from the story you have been telling about your own past.

The remembered past is not the actual past. It is a present construction of what the past was.

This is not a failure of memory. It is what memory is.

The past, as experienced in memory, is always filtered through the present self doing the remembering.

Proust:

Marcel Proust spent 3,000 pages exploring this in In Search of Lost Time —

the madeleine dipped in tea that suddenly releases a memory not as a record but as a full sensory re-experience —

involuntary memory — the past that comes unbidden, more vivid than voluntary recollection —

and the narrator’s realization that what he was searching for — lost time — could only be recovered in art, which makes the past permanent by making it the present of the reader.

“The true paradises are the paradises we have lost.”

The lost past is more real than the present — because in the present, we are too close to see it.

Only in memory, only in art that activates memory, does experience become fully itself.

The physics of memory:

Memory is encoded in synaptic connections — in the physical structure of the brain.

When you remember, neurons fire in patterns that recreate the original pattern.

But: each time you remember, the memory is slightly altered — reconsolidated with new information.

The memory of a memory is not the original experience. It is the memory of the last time you remembered it.

The past, neurologically, is continuously rewritten in the present.

VI. The Future — Expectation and Anxiety

Augustine: the present of future things is expectation.

The future does not exist. And yet we live oriented toward it.

Every action is directed toward a future state. Every plan assumes a future. Every hope, every fear, every intention — all are directed toward what is not yet.

Heidegger’s Entwurf — projection — the way Dasein always throws itself ahead toward possibilities.

We are not beings who have a future. We are beings who are always already projecting ourselves toward a future — who exist in the mode of the not-yet.

The tyranny of the future:

The future that concerns us most is the one we cannot plan for: death.

The awareness of mortality — explicit or implicit — shapes the entire temporal horizon of experience.

Everything is done in the light of finitude. Every choice involves the other choices that are thereby not made. Every moment contains the mortality that makes it this moment and not an infinite succession of others.

This is not morbidity. It is the condition of seriousness.

The person who genuinely lives in the awareness of finitude takes their time seriously — knows that this moment is not replaceable, that this conversation, this decision, this experience — will not come again.

The person who lives in denial of finitude can always defer — there will be time later, another chance, a tomorrow that is structurally identical to today.

Death cancels the infinite deferral. It makes the present the only time there is.

VII. The Eternal Present

Against the rushing of time — the Buddhist, the Stoic, the contemplative traditions of many cultures have proposed a different relationship with time:

The eternal present.

Not eternity as infinite duration — time going on forever.

But eternity as the depth of the present — the present moment experienced in its fullness, without the pull of past or future.

Marcus Aurelius:

“Confine yourself to the present.”

Not as a counsel of forgetfulness — but as a recognition that the past and future are not available to us.

Only the present is real. Only the present can be inhabited. The mind that is always elsewhere — in the regretted past, in the anxious future — is never where it is.

The physics of the present:

In special relativity: the present — the “now” — is relative. Different observers have different nows.

Is there any absolute present?

The light cone of an event: the set of all events that could be causally connected — by signals traveling at most at the speed of light.

The absolute present — the set of events spacelike separated from here-now — is not causally connected to here-now. It cannot influence it. It cannot be influenced by it.

In a precise physical sense: the only “present” that exists is the infinitesimally thin slice of the light cone vertex.

Everything else is past or future.

The eternal present of contemplative traditions — the depth of the now — corresponds, oddly, to the physically real present: the knife-edge of the light cone.

What the mystic reaches for in meditation — the nowness of now — is, physically, the only moment there is.

VIII. Time and Language

Wittgenstein noted: the grammar of our language generates philosophical puzzles about time.

“Time passes” — but what is the subject? What is doing the passing?

“The same moment” — what makes moments the same or different?

“Now” — the most indexical word in language, pointing to the present — cannot be given a description that is not itself indexical.

Every attempt to describe “now” uses another word that refers to now.

The grammar of temporal language is not a description of time. It is a form of life — a set of practices for coordinating human activity in time.

When we ask “what is time?” we may be asking: what is the grammar of temporal language?

And the answer may be: there is no deeper answer. The grammar is what it is. The practices are what they are.

Time is what we do with it.

IX. What We Don’t Know

After Augustine, after Heidegger, after Einstein, after quantum mechanics —

what do we know about time?

We know:

Time dilates with velocity and gravity. The past and future are equally real in the block universe interpretation. The arrow of time comes from entropy. Time had a beginning — the Big Bang. Below the Planck time, time has no meaning.

We don’t know:

Whether time flows or is static. Whether the present is real or relative. Whether time is continuous or discrete. Whether time had a beginning in any absolute sense. What time was “before” the Big Bang — or whether “before” applies. Why time has a direction at all. Whether time is fundamental or emergent.

The deepest mystery:

Why does time seem to flow?

In the block universe, all moments equally exist. Nothing moves. Nothing passes. The universe is a static four-dimensional structure.

And yet: you experience time as flowing. The present feels different from the past. The future feels open in a way the past does not.

Why?

This question — the question of why we experience time as flowing in a world where physics suggests nothing flows —

is the hardest question in the philosophy of time.

It connects physics to consciousness. It connects the structure of the world to the structure of experience. It has no agreed answer.

X. Living in Time

All of this philosophy, all of this physics — and time remains what it was before any of it:

The medium of your life.

The stuff you are made of. The dimension in which everything happens — everything you remember, everything you anticipate, everything you are doing right now.

Augustine’s restless heart. Heidegger’s being-toward-death. Einstein’s dilating clocks. Proust’s involuntary memory. The Stoic’s present moment.

All of these are attempts to understand the same thing:

What it is to be a being who exists in time — who is constituted by past, oriented toward future, and always already in the present that passes before it can be held.

The answer is not available. The question is indispensable.

“Si nemo ex me quaerat, scio.”

If no one asks me, I know.

I know it right now — this moment, this passing, this movement from what was to what will be through the knife-edge of what is.

I know it in the way I know how to breathe.

And when I try to explain it —

I do not know.

Further reading: Augustine — Confessions (397) (trans. Henry Chadwick — Oxford World’s Classics) — Book XI for the philosophy of time Heidegger — Being and Time (1927) (trans. Macquarrie & Robinson, or Stambaugh) — Division Two for temporality and death Husserl — On the Phenomenology of the Consciousness of Internal Time (1928) (trans. Brough — Kluwer) — the technical foundation McTaggart — “The Unreality of Time” (1908) Mind 17, 457 — the A-series/B-series distinction; essential Price — Time’s Arrow and Archimedes’ Point (1996) Oxford University Press — the physics of time’s direction Barbour — The End of Time (1999) Oxford University Press — the block universe; time as illusion Rovelli — The Order of Time (2018) — the most beautiful popular account Proust — In Search of Lost Time (trans. Moncrieff & Kilmartin) — 3,000 pages on memory and time; worth every one For the physics: Price — Time’s Arrow (above) Penrose — The Road to Reality (2004) — Chapters 27-28 for time asymmetry
entry.summary: The most intimate and most mysterious of all phenomena. We live inside it. We cannot step outside it to examine it. And the closer we look, the stranger it becomes.

entry.title: Bivariate Bicycle Codes — A New Hope for qLDPC

link.title: Bivariate Bicycle Codes — A New Hope for qLDPC

entry.content:
qLDPC codes have been theoretically promising for years. The question was always: can we find explicit constructions with good parameters and tractable decoding and some hope of physical implementation?

Bivariate Bicycle (BB) codes, introduced by Bravyi et al. (2024), are a serious answer to that question.

Construction

BB codes are defined over the group algebra of \(\mathbb{Z}_l \times \mathbb{Z}_m\).

Let \(x\) and \(y\) be the cyclic shift operators on \(\mathbb{Z}_l\) and \(\mathbb{Z}_m\) respectively. Choose two polynomials:

\[A(x,y), \quad B(x,y) \in \mathbb{F}_2[x,y] / (x^l - 1, y^m - 1)\]

The parity check matrices are:

\[H_X = [A \mid B], \qquad H_Z = [B^T \mid A^T]\]

This gives a CSS code on \(n = 2lm\) physical qubits.

The commutativity condition \(H_X H_Z^T = 0\) is automatically satisfied by construction — a clean algebraic consequence of the bicyclic structure.

Why BB Codes Are Interesting

1. High encoding rate.

Surface code encodes \(k=1\) logical qubit into \(n \sim d^2\) physical qubits. BB codes achieve \(k = 2 \cdot \text{rank}(\ker A \cap \ker B^T)\), which for well-chosen polynomials gives \(k \sim \Theta(n)\).

The landmark example from Bravyi et al.: [[144, 12, 12]] — 144 physical qubits encoding 12 logical qubits at distance 12. Surface code needs ~1728 physical qubits for the same parameters.

2. Low-weight stabilizers.

All stabilizers have weight exactly 6 — three from \(A\), three from \(B\). This is a hard constraint from the polynomial structure, and it’s what makes BB codes physically plausible. Compare to hypergraph product codes where stabilizer weights grow with \(n\).

3. Quasi-cyclic structure.

The cyclic structure enables efficient syndrome extraction circuits and makes BP-OSD decoding particularly effective on these codes.

The Polynomial Choice Problem

Here’s what Bravyi et al. didn’t fully answer:

Given a group \(\mathbb{Z}_l \times \mathbb{Z}_m\), which polynomial pairs \((A, B)\) maximize the distance \(d\)?

Shaw & Terhal (2024) explored this more systematically, introducing the morphing circuit framework and identifying high-performance BB codes in several group sizes.

But their search was not exhaustive. The polynomial space is large, and distance computation is expensive — exact distance for a [[72, 12, ?]] code requires solving a minimum-weight codeword problem that is NP-hard in general.

So the question remains open: Are there BB codes with better parameters than currently known?

What I’m Working On

Systematic search in groups Shaw & Terhal didn’t fully explore.

For \(\mathbb{Z}_{12} \times \mathbb{Z}_6\) — \(n = 144\) physical qubits — I’ve been running exhaustive searches over polynomial pairs that include cross-terms: \(x^i y^j\) monomials Shaw restricted away.

Early results: several [[72, 12, 7]] codes with polynomial choices outside Shaw’s search space.

The trade-off: cross-term polynomials yield stabilizer weight 11 instead of Shaw’s weight 9 — more CNOT gates, harder circuits. Whether the distance gain justifies the circuit cost depends on the physical error model.

That’s the next question.

More soon. 🙂
entry.summary: Structure, parameters, and why they matter.

entry.title: The Art of Folding — Paper, History, and the Mathematics of Modular Origami

link.title: The Art of Folding — Paper, History, and the Mathematics of Modular Origami

entry.content:
There is a moment, when folding paper, where something shifts.

The material stops being passive. It begins to speak — in angles, in tensions, in the geometry of a crease that, once made, cannot be entirely unmade.

I have been folding since I was a child. Modular origami — the kind where dozens or hundreds of units interlock without glue into a single structure — is the form I keep returning to.

Not because it is the most beautiful. Not always. But because it is the most honest: every unit must be identical, every join must be precise, and the whole will not hold unless everything is right.

There is no hiding in modular origami.

I. The Invention of Paper

To understand origami, you must first understand paper.

Before paper, writing surfaces were expensive, heavy, fragile, or scarce. Clay tablets. Papyrus. Silk. Bamboo strips. None of these fold.

Paper — thin, flexible, cheap, strong — changed everything.

The invention is attributed to Cai Lun (蔡倫), a court official of the Han dynasty, who in 105 CE presented Emperor He with a new writing material made from bark, hemp, rags, and fishnets.

This is the traditional account. Archaeological evidence suggests papermaking existed in China perhaps two centuries earlier — fragments have been found dating to the Western Han period (206 BCE – 9 CE). But Cai Lun standardized and improved the process, and the court’s adoption of his method spread it throughout the empire.

The process he developed is recognizably modern:

Fibrous plant material — bark of mulberry, hemp, bamboo — is soaked, beaten into a pulp, suspended in water, and scooped onto a flat screen. As the water drains, the fibers bond. The sheet is pressed and dried.

The result: a material that is simultaneously strong and flexible, light and durable, and — crucially for origami — capable of holding a crease.

II. Paper Travels West

From China, papermaking spread along trade routes with extraordinary slowness by modern standards — and extraordinary speed by ancient ones.

Korea received papermaking in the 4th century CE. Korean paper — hanji (한지) — made from the bark of the mulberry tree (Broussonetia papyrifera) became famous for its exceptional durability. Documents written on hanji have survived a thousand years.

Japan received the technology in 610 CE, according to traditional accounts via the Buddhist monk Damjing from Korea. Japanese papermaking developed into an art form of its own — producing papers of extraordinary variety, texture, and beauty.

The Islamic world acquired papermaking after the Battle of Talas (751 CE), where Arab forces captured Chinese papermakers. Paper mills appeared in Samarkand, then Baghdad, then across the Islamic world. The Arabic word for paper — waraq — entered dozens of languages.

Europe received paper through the Islamic world, with the first European paper mills appearing in Spain and Italy in the 12th and 13th centuries.

By the time Gutenberg invented movable type in 1440, paper was ready for it.

III. Japanese Paper — Washi

The Japanese word for paper is kami (紙). It shares its sound — though not its character — with the word for spirit or god: kami (神).

This is not coincidence, or rather, it is the kind of coincidence that shapes a culture. Paper in Japan was sacred material. It was used in Shinto rituals, folded into ritual forms called shide that still hang in shrines today.

Japanese papermaking — washi (和紙, wa = Japanese, shi = paper) — developed its own distinct tradition.

The finest washi papers are still made by hand, using traditional techniques that have changed little in a thousand years.

Among the most celebrated:

Washi (和紙) — the general term for traditional Japanese handmade paper, made primarily from three plants: kōzo (楮, mulberry), mitsumata (三椏), and gampi (雁皮). Each produces paper with distinct qualities — kōzo is strong and flexible, gampi is smooth and lustrous.

Yuzen (友禅) — not strictly a paper type but a dyeing technique, originally developed for silk kimono fabric in the Edo period by the fan painter Miyazaki Yūzen. The technique — using rice paste as a resist to create precise, elaborate patterns — was later applied to paper. Yuzen paper is used in bookbinding, crafts, and high-end origami.

Chiyogami (千代紙) — chiyо means “thousand generations,” gami is the voiced form of kami (paper). Traditional Japanese decorative paper, featuring bold, repetitive patterns derived from kimono fabric designs. Originally produced in Kyoto and Edo. The patterns — cranes, waves, cherry blossoms, geometric forms — are printed using hand-carved woodblocks. Used in traditional crafts and ceremonial wrapping.

Kozo (楮紙) — pure mulberry paper, prized for strength. Used in conservation and restoration of artworks worldwide. The Library of Congress uses kozo paper to repair ancient documents.

Momigami (揉み紙) — paper that has been crumpled and worked by hand until it achieves a soft, leather-like texture. Used in decorative arts and certain origami techniques.

IV. The Word Origami

Origami (折り紙) — ori (折り) meaning “folding,” kami (紙) meaning “paper” — voiced to gami in compound.

The word is surprisingly recent.

The practice of paper folding in Japan is ancient — at least as old as the introduction of paper itself. But for most of Japanese history, the craft was called orikata (折り形, “folded shapes”) or orisue (折り据え).

The word origami in its current sense became standard only in the modern period — some scholars date its widespread use to the Meiji era (1868–1912) or even later.

Before this, two distinct traditions existed:

Noshi (熨斗) — formal paper folding for ceremonial use. Paper folded around gifts, used in wedding ceremonies, attached to sake cups. These were rigid, prescribed forms — not art but ritual.

Tsutsumi (包み) — wrapping and folding paper for gifts and formal presentation. The aesthetics of wrapping — the geometry of folded paper — were codified into elaborate systems of etiquette.

The tradition of folding paper for play and artistic expression — closer to what we now call origami — developed alongside these ceremonial forms and was documented in the 18th century work Hiden Senbazuru Orikata (秘伝千羽鶴折形, 1797): “The Secret of One Thousand Cranes” — the first known book of origami instructions.

V. The Crane and the Legend

Senbazuru — one thousand cranes.

The tradition: fold one thousand paper cranes, and your wish will be granted. Some versions say: you will be healed of illness. Some say: you will live for a thousand years.

The legend became inseparable from Sadako Sasaki — a girl who was two years old when the atomic bomb fell on Hiroshima in 1945.

She developed leukemia at twelve. She began folding cranes in the hospital, aiming for one thousand.

She folded 644 before she died. Her classmates completed the remaining 356.

She was twelve years old.

There is a statue of Sadako in the Hiroshima Peace Memorial Park. She holds a golden crane. Every year, thousands of children from around the world send origami cranes to be placed at her memorial.

This is what paper can hold.

VI. The Mathematics of Folding

For most of its history, origami was transmitted as craft — through instruction, imitation, practice.

In the 20th century, mathematicians began to ask: what are the underlying principles?

The result was a new field: computational origami — the mathematics of paper folding.

Huzita-Hatori axioms:

In 1989, Humiaki Huzita formalized the operations possible with a single fold. Later extended by Koshiro Hatori, the complete set of seven axioms defines what can be constructed by folding.

Remarkably: origami is more powerful than compass-and-straightedge construction. With origami, you can:

  • Trisect an angle (impossible with compass and straightedge)
  • Double a cube (impossible with compass and straightedge)
  • Solve certain cubic equations

The fold is a more powerful geometric operation than the compass arc or the straight line.

Flat-foldability:

When does a crease pattern fold flat?

Two necessary and sufficient conditions for a single vertex:

  1. Kawasaki’s theorem: At any interior vertex, the alternating sum of angles equals zero: \(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2 + \alpha_3 - \alpha_4 + \cdots = 0\)

  2. Maekawa’s theorem: At any interior vertex, the number of mountain folds and valley folds differ by exactly two: \(|M - V| = 2\)

These are local conditions — they apply vertex by vertex. Global flat-foldability (whether the entire model folds flat without self-intersection) is a harder problem. It has been proven NP-hard in general.

VII. The Miura Fold

In 1970, Japanese astrophysicist Kōryō Miura developed a folding pattern for deploying large structures in space.

The Miura fold (ミウラ折り) is a method of folding a flat surface into a smaller area using a tessellation of parallelograms.

Its key property: it has one degree of freedom. The entire surface can be opened or closed with a single motion — pulled at opposite corners, the whole sheet expands. Released, it collapses.

No sequential unfolding required. No risk of a panel failing to deploy.

The Miura fold was used in the solar panels of Japan’s Space Flyer Unit (1995). It appears in maps designed to be opened and closed with one hand. It has been proposed for airbags, medical stents, architectural facades.

The fold obeys a beautiful mechanical property:

In the Miura fold, each parallelogram unit is a rigid panel. The folding motion is distributed entirely in the creases. This means the fold can be made from rigid materials — metal, carbon fiber — not just paper.

Origami geometry becoming aerospace engineering.

VIII. Rigid Origami and Deployable Structures

The Miura fold is one instance of a broader field: rigid origami — folding patterns where the faces between creases undergo no bending, and all deformation is concentrated in the crease lines.

This has profound engineering applications.

Heart stents: A flat mesh folded into a small cylinder for insertion, then deployed to hold open a coronary artery. The geometry of the fold must be precisely engineered to achieve the required expansion ratio without tearing.

Space telescopes: The James Webb Space Telescope’s primary mirror — 6.5 meters in diameter — was folded to fit inside the Ariane 5 rocket fairing and deployed in space. The folding sequence was engineered over years.

Soft robotics: Origami-inspired structures that can change shape in response to pneumatic pressure, used in grippers, locomotion systems, and medical devices.

Architecture: Building facades that adjust to light and temperature using origami-inspired kinetic panels.

The mathematics of paper folding, developed in the context of an ancient craft, is now a branch of mechanical engineering.

IX. Modular Origami — The Form I Love

Standard origami produces a single form from a single sheet. Modular origami builds a structure from multiple identical units — modules — that interlock without adhesive.

The history of modular origami is relatively recent.

The earliest known modular form is the Sonobe unit, developed by Mitsunobu Sonobe in the late 1960s. A single Sonobe unit is a simple parallelogram-based form. But 12 units assemble into a small stellated dodecahedron. 30 units into an icosahedron. 270 units into a large stellated truncated icosahedron.

The three-dimensional modulars that followed — developed by Tomoko Fuse, Robert Neale, and later the extraordinary Ekaterina Lukasheva — pushed the form into territory that feels less like craft and more like crystallography.

Why modular origami is mathematically deep:

Each unit must satisfy local constraints: the tabs must fit the pockets precisely, the angles must be consistent, the tension must be distributed evenly.

For large models, this becomes a global constraint satisfaction problem.

Consider a 180-unit buckyball — a truncated icosahedron, the shape of a football / soccer ball. 60 pentagonal faces, 12 hexagonal faces. Every unit must be identically folded. Every join must be identically tensioned. The whole structure is held by friction and geometry alone.

If a single unit is folded incorrectly — if a single angle is off by a few degrees — the error propagates. The structure refuses to close.

There is no hiding in modular origami.

X. The Geometry of Assembly

Modular origami maps onto polyhedral geometry.

The most common forms are based on the Platonic solids — the five convex regular polyhedra known since antiquity:

  • Tetrahedron (4 triangular faces)
  • Cube (6 square faces)
  • Octahedron (8 triangular faces)
  • Dodecahedron (12 pentagonal faces)
  • Icosahedron (20 triangular faces)

And the Archimedean solids — 13 convex polyhedra with regular polygonal faces of two or more types, the same arrangement of faces at every vertex.

The truncated icosahedron — 12 pentagons, 20 hexagons — is the buckyball, the soccer ball, and one of the most beautiful modular origami targets.

Euler’s formula governs all of these:

\[V - E + F = 2\]

Vertices minus edges plus faces equals two. For any convex polyhedron. Always.

When you assemble a modular origami structure, you are physically instantiating this theorem. Every vertex, every edge, every face — your hands have placed them.

XI. Paper as Thought

I want to end not with mathematics but with phenomenology.

There is something that happens when you fold.

The mind quiets. Not because the task is mindless — it is not. Precision is required constantly. But because the precision is physical, not verbal. You are not thinking in words. You are thinking in angles.

The paper has its own logic. It tells you, through resistance and compliance, when a fold is right and when it is wrong. You learn to listen to it.

Modular origami extends this across time.

A large model — 90 units, 180 units, 270 units — takes hours, sometimes days. Each unit is folded identically. The repetition is not tedious. It is meditative. Your hands learn the form so well that by the hundredth unit, you are no longer thinking about it.

And then the assembly.

The units that were separate become a whole. The structure that existed only as a diagram — or as an image in your mind — becomes physical, becomes holdable, becomes real.

This is not a metaphor for anything. It is itself.

A thing made of paper and time and attention, held together by geometry and friction, that did not exist before you made it.

That is enough.

If you want to start: Learn the Sonobe unit first — 12 units, one afternoon. Then Tomoko Fuse’s Unit Origami (1990) for intermediate modular forms. For the mathematics: Robert Lang’s Origami Design Secrets (2003) is the most complete treatment available. For inspiration: search Ekaterina Lukasheva’s work. Prepare to lose several days.
entry.summary: From Cai Lun's invention to Miura folds — everything that happens when paper meets intention.

entry.title: The Game of Go — The Most Human Game Ever Invented

link.title: The Game of Go — The Most Human Game Ever Invented

entry.content:
I am a 6-dan Go player.

I say this not to impress — in East Asia, where Go was born, 6-dan is a serious amateur level, not a professional title. There are players who have dedicated their entire lives to this game and will still lose to a 9-dan professional by 50 points.

That humility is, perhaps, the first thing Go teaches you.

What Is Go?

The rules of Go are deceptively simple.

Two players — Black and White — alternate placing stones on the intersections of a 19×19 grid. Stones surrounded on all sides by the opponent are captured and removed. The player who controls more territory at the end wins.

That’s it. No dice. No hidden information. No luck.

And yet — Go has more possible board positions than there are atoms in the observable universe.

\[\text{Possible Go games} \approx 10^{700}\]

Chess, by comparison: \(10^{120}\).

The difference is not incremental. It is categorical.

Weiqi — The Chinese Origin

Go is at least 2,500 years old. Its Chinese name is Weiqi (围棋) — literally “encirclement game.”

In ancient China, Weiqi was one of the four arts of the scholar-gentleman, alongside calligraphy, painting, and music — qín, qí, shū, huà (琴棋書畫).

To not know Weiqi was to be considered uncultured.

The game was not merely entertainment — it was a model for military strategy, political thinking, philosophical inquiry. Sun Tzu’s Art of War and Weiqi share a deep vocabulary: encirclement, sacrifice, the value of position over material.

A general who could not play Weiqi was thought to lack the strategic mind required for command.

Baduk — Korea’s Obsession

Go arrived in Korea around the 5th century CE, where it became Baduk (바둑).

Korean Go culture is arguably the most intense in the world. Professional players begin training at age 6–8, spending 8–10 hours a day studying for years before their first professional match.

The Korean Baduk TV channel broadcasts Go 24 hours a day. Professional matches are covered like major sporting events. Top players are celebrities.

Korea dominated world Go from the 1980s through the 2010s — players like Lee Changho, Choi Cheolhan, and later Park Junghwan were the Michael Jordans of the Go world.

Igo — Japan’s Aesthetic

In Japan, Go is Igo (囲碁), and it carries a different weight.

Japanese Go culture elevated the game to an art form. The aesthetic of the game — the sound of a stone placed on a wooden board, the feel of a slate-and-shell stone between the fingers, the silence between moves — all of this matters.

The traditional equipment is exquisite: Kaya wood boards, kugeishi (slate) black stones, hamaguri (clam shell) white stones. A full professional-grade set can cost tens of thousands of dollars.

The Japanese Go world also gave us the concept of fuseki — opening theory — and joseki — corner sequences — which are studied the way musicians study Bach.

Honinbo Shusaku, a 19th century Japanese professional, is considered by many the greatest player in history before the AI era. His style — patient, territorial, deep — influenced the game for 150 years.

AlphaGo — The Day Everything Changed

In March 2016, DeepMind’s AlphaGo defeated Lee Sedol — arguably the greatest human player of his generation — 4–1 in a five-game match.

The Go world was shaken.

Not because AI defeated a human — chess had fallen to Deep Blue in 1997. But because of how AlphaGo played.

Its moves were strange. Inhuman. Wrong-looking. And then, ten moves later, you understood.

Move 37 in Game 2 — a shoulder hit on the fifth line — was described by commentators as “not a human move.” Lee Sedol left the room for fifteen minutes after seeing it. He later said it made him question his entire understanding of the game.

AlphaGo used a combination of deep convolutional neural networks and Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS):

  • A policy network trained on millions of human games, predicting which moves are worth considering.
  • A value network estimating the probability of winning from any given board position.
  • MCTS to search forward, guided by both networks.

AlphaGo Zero (2017) went further — trained entirely through self-play, with no human game data at all. Starting from random play, it surpassed AlphaGo in three days. It discovered joseki that humans had developed over centuries — and then discarded some of them as suboptimal.

KataGo (2019–present), an open-source successor, is now available to everyone. Playing against KataGo at full strength is like staring into an abyss that stares back with perfect clarity.

What AI Taught Human Players

Here is the remarkable thing:

After AlphaGo, human Go improved.

AI analysis tools showed that many established joseki were wrong. Opening theories developed over decades were revised. Moves once considered amateur mistakes were revealed as genius.

The AI didn’t end Go. It deepened it.

Today, top professionals use AI analysis the way musicians use recordings — to study, to question, to improve. The game is richer now than it was before 2016.

Why You Should Learn Go

Chess is a game of elimination. You win by destroying.

Go is a game of coexistence — until it isn’t. You build, you surround, you sacrifice, you invade. The balance shifts move by move.

A single game of Go can contain: a catastrophic blunder that turns into a brilliant sacrifice, a territory you thought was safe that suddenly collapses, a group you thought was dead that comes back to life.

Go teaches you that:

  • Losing is information. Every loss shows you something you didn’t see.
  • Efficiency matters more than force. One stone in the right place beats ten stones in the wrong place.
  • Local and global are always in tension. Winning a corner can lose the game.
  • Patience is a weapon.

I have been playing for years, and I am still learning how to lose well.

That, more than anything, is why I keep playing.

If you want to start: download KGS or OGS (online Go servers), pick up a 9×9 board, and play your first hundred games without worrying about winning.

The game will teach you the rest.
entry.summary: On Weiqi, AlphaGo, and why 19×19 intersections contain everything.

entry.title: Süperpozisyon ve Dolanıklık
entry.content:
Klasik fizik deterministtir. Bir topun nerede olduğunu, nereye gideceğini, Newton’un denklemleriyle tam olarak hesaplarsın. Evren bir saat gibi işler.

Sonra kuantum geldi ve her şeyi yerle bir etti.

Süperpozisyon: Belirsizlik bir hata değil

Bir elektronu düşün. Spin ölçmek istiyorsun — yukarı mı, aşağı mı?

Klasik sezgi der ki: “Zaten bir değeri vardır, sen sadece bilmiyorsun.” Kuantum mekaniği der ki: Hayır. Ölçene kadar ikisi de.

Bu epistemik bir belirsizlik değil — yani “bilgimiz yetersiz” değil. Ontolojik bir belirsizlik — gerçekliğin kendisi belirsiz.

Bunu kanıtlayan deney var: Bell eşitsizlikleri. 1964’te John Bell teorik olarak gösterdi, 1982’de Aspect deneyleriyle doğrulandı:

Eğer parçacıkların gizli, önceden belirlenmiş değerleri olsaydı, belirli korelasyon ölçümleri Bell sınırını aşamazdı. Aştılar.

Yani evren gerçekten belirsiz. Lokal gizli değişkenler yok.

Dolanıklık: Einstein’ı rahatsız eden şey

İki parçacık dolanık hale getirildiğinde — birinin spinini ölçtüğünde, diğerinin spini anında belirleniyor. Aralarında ışık yılları olsa bile.

Einstein buna “spukhafte Fernwirkung” dedi — uzaktan ürkütücü etki. Kabul etmek istemedi. EPR paradoksuyla çürütmeye çalıştı.

Ama Bell ve Aspect onu yanılttı!

Peki bu bilgi ışıktan hızlı mı gidiyor? Hayır. Çünkü hangi sonucun geleceğini kontrol edemiyorsun. Rastgele. Dolayısıyla bilgi iletimi yok, görelilik korunuyor.

Ama korelasyon gerçek. Lokal değil. Evrensel.

Şimdi ne?

Süperpozisyon ve dolanıklık artık sadece teori değil — kuantum bilgisayarların temeli.

Kuantum bitleri, yani qubit’ler, süperpozisyonda hesap yapıyor. Dolanıklık ise qubit’ler arasında klasik iletişim olmadan korelasyon kuruyor — bu da bazı algoritmaları klasik bilgisayarların erişemeyeceği hızlara taşıyor.

Ama qubit’lerin bir sorunu var: gürültü. Süperpozisyon kırılgan. Çevre etkileşimleri onu çökertir — buna decoherence diyoruz.

Ve ben tam da bu sorunla uğraşıyorum. Ama o ayrı bir yazı. 🙂
entry.summary: Kuantum mekaniğinin en tuhaf iki gerçeği üzerine.
